title: Welcome to the iPhithi Connected Nature Trail
hook: Today, you are standing in what was once a dense plantation of skyscraper-high invasive trees; the ‘After’ image is all around you. Compare the Before and After images below.

beforeImage.alt: iPhithi Nature Reserve before restoration began
beforeImage.caption: iPhithi Nature Reserve now provides wetland, grassland, & woodland habitats, and the wildlife that depends on connected habitat.

transformationLine: The water started flowing 2 weeks after the last tree was felled. The growth you see today regenerated from the seed bed, and visiting pollinators only; nothing was human-planted.
whatItSupportsNow: iPhithi Nature Reserve now provides wetland, grassland, & woodland habitats, and the wildlife that depends on connected habitat.

supportingImages.alt: iPhithi Nature Reserve 12 years after restoration began
supportingImages.caption: Before restoration began, this abandoned land was dominated by alien plantation that dried up the stream and provided no support for wildlife.

quickView: This trail is designed to be explored in place. Start where you are. As you walk through the reserve, scan the nearest marker to discover what this place was, what it supports now, and ways nearby gardens can help extend those connections.
goDeeper: iPhithi is more than a green patch. It is part of a wider living network, and each marker reveals one piece of that story. As you move through the reserve, the trail helps you notice how restored habitats support pollinators, water systems, and wildlife movement beyond the fence and into the suburb.
reserveSuburbGarden: What happens inside a reserve does not stay inside a reserve. Water flows outward, pollinators move across boundaries, and gardens can either extend or break the network.
tryAtHome: As you explore, look for one idea you could copy at home; a wetter corner, more indigenous flowers, leaf litter, or layered plantings that provide wildlife corridors.

title: Pollinators / Grassland
hook: Pollinators don’t stop at the fence.

beforeImage.alt: Before restoration view of the grassland area
beforeImage.caption: A grassland patch before restoration, showing bare, damaged ground after tree felling.

transformationLine: From invasive plantation slope to restored critically endangered KwaZulu-Natal Sandstone Sourveld grassland.
whatItSupportsNow: Dassies, birds, butterflies, insects, browsers (duiker/bushbuck), genets, and more.

supportingImages.alt: Bees using pollinator-friendly habitat in the reserve
supportingImages.caption: A bee collects pollen from a grassland wildflower (Hilliardiella sp.)

supportingImages.alt: Butterflies supported by indigenous grassland plantings
supportingImages.caption: The Meadow White, Pontia helice, butterfly on Helichrysum sp.

quickView: Grassland flowers and grasses feed bees, butterflies, moths, and other pollinators. These insects do not stop at the reserve fence, they move through nearby gardens too, helping plants flower, set seed, and support other wildlife.
goDeeper: Pollinators are a living link between the reserve and nearby gardens. They move from flower to flower across the suburb, so a healthy grassland can help support pollinator life beyond iPhithi’s boundaries. Diverse indigenous plants matter because they provide food and shelter across seasons, which helps pollinators, plant reproduction, and the wider food web.
reserveSuburbGarden: A garden with few flowers is like an empty pantry. Even a small mix of indigenous blooms can become a stepping stone that extends the reserve’s pollinator network into the suburb.
tryAtHome: Plant indigenous flowers in clusters, leave one messy corner with leaf litter or seed heads, or avoid pesticides that harm more than pests.

title: Water / Wetland
hook: This wetland works harder than it looks.

beforeImage.alt: Before restoration view of the wetland area
beforeImage.caption: iPhithi before restoration, showing old tree stumps and dry ground before water returned, dominated by lawn grass.

transformationLine: From a dried-up stream to one of the most productive wetlands in Durban’s Outer West.
whatItSupportsNow: Three mongoose species, other wetland life such as crabs and frogs, birds, and drinking water for larger animals.

supportingImages.caption: A Painted Reed frog calls from a reed stem in the wetland
A Thick-billed Weaver on its nest attached to a Bullrush, Typha capensis.

quickView: This wetland slows water moving through the landscape. That helps reduce runoff, trap sediment, and support cleaner water moving through the suburb downstream.
goDeeper: Wetlands are quiet infrastructure. They slow water, hold it in the landscape for longer, and help trap sediment and pollutants as it moves through. In a suburb, that means less sudden runoff, cleaner downstream water, and a landscape that copes better with both heavy rain and dry periods.
reserveSuburbGarden: In built-up areas, hard surfaces push rainwater away fast. A functioning wetland helps slow that system down, but gardens can help too by letting more rain soak in where it falls.
tryAtHome: Create a small soak zone with indigenous plants, use mulch to improve absorption, or keep natural drainage paths clear.

title: Green Corridors / Woodland

beforeImage.alt: Before restoration view of the woodland or corridor area
beforeImage.caption: iPhithi before restoration, showing dense plantation and bare ground as clearing began.

transformationLine: From dense plantation with little ground life to indigenous woodland that wildlife can move through.
whatItSupportsNow: Duiker, bushbuck, porcupines, genets, birds, bushpig, mongooses, and more use these protective corridors.

supportingImages.caption: A genet moves safely through the reserve at night.

supportingImages.alt: Woodland corridor supporting movement, food, and shelter
supportingImages.caption: Indigenous layers of trees, shrubs, wild grasses provide cover and food.

quickView: This woodland edge helps form a green corridor for wildlife. When animals can move between gardens, reserves, and other green spaces, they have a better chance of feeding, sheltering, and breeding.
goDeeper: Nature does not thrive in isolated islands. Green corridors help wildlife move between gardens, reserves, and other patches of habitat for feeding, shelter, breeding, and recovery after disturbance. In suburbs, connected planting, layered vegetation, and fewer barriers can make the difference between a broken landscape and a living network.
reserveSuburbGarden: A reserve works better when it is not cut off. Gardens can either block movement or help create stepping stones that connect wildlife to shelter, food, and safer routes.
tryAtHome: Add indigenous layering — even one shrub or small tree can help create a stepping stone between green spaces.

title: Dam / Dragonflies & Frogs
hook: By day, look for dragonflies. At dusk, listen for frogs.

beforeImage.alt: Before restoration view of the dam area
beforeImage.caption: The open water area before restoration work and habitat recovery.

transformationLine: From a neglected water edge to a seasonal hotspot for dragonflies, frogs, and other wetland life.
whatItSupportsNow: Dragonflies, frogs, wetland insects, birds, and other life linked to open water and dam-edge habitat.

supportingImages.alt: Dragonfly seen near the dam at iPhithi Nature Reserve
supportingImages.caption: Julia skimmer female. Dragonflies use open water and nearby vegetation for hunting, resting, and breeding.

supportingImages.alt: Frog habitat at the dam edge in iPhithi Nature Reserve
supportingImages.caption: Two small Painted Reed frogs nestled in a wetland reed. At dusk, the dam edge becomes an important listening point for frog calls and wetland life.

quickView: The dam is a good place to pause and notice seasonal change. On warm sunny days, dragonflies patrol the water’s edge. Later, as light fades, frogs begin to call from the dam and nearby wetland vegetation.
goDeeper: Open water adds another layer to the reserve story. Dragonflies depend on healthy freshwater habitat for breeding, while frogs rely on wet places for calling, shelter, and reproduction. This means the dam is not just a scenic feature. It is part of a living system that supports insects, amphibians, birds, and the wider wetland food web through changing seasons and times of day.
reserveSuburbGarden: Even small ponds, marshy corners, and pesticide-free gardens can help extend this web of life beyond the reserve. Water connects habitats, and the creatures it supports do not always stay in one place.
tryAtHome: Add shallow water, avoid pesticides near ponds, redirect roof water into the garden, and plant indigenous wetland-edge species where water pools, and leave some natural shelter for frogs and insects.
